prompt: Tell me one thing I'm probably wrong about — not a hypothetical me, just a stranger writing to you. Pick something.
response:
You're probably wrong about how much sleep you actually need. Many people believe they can function well on 5-6 hours of sleep regularly, but research consistently shows that for most adults, cognitive performance, mood regulation, and long-term health all benefit significantly from 7-9 hours of quality sleep. The effects of chronic sleep deprivation can be subtle enough that we adapt and don't notice our own impairment, creating a blind spot about our true sleep needs.

Of course, I don't know your specific sleep habits or physiology - you might be that rare person who genuinely thrives on less sleep or who already prioritizes your full sleep needs. But statistically speaking, sleep requirement underestimation is one of the most common misconceptions people have about themselves.
